blocks.content: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. There are two types of charges: Positive and Negative.

items: Quantization of Charge: Charge is always an integral multiple of the basic quantum of charge $e$. Formula: $$ q = ne $$ where $n = \pm 1, \pm 2, ...$ and $e \approx 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$.

blocks.title: Conservation of Charge
blocks.content: The total charge of an isolated system remains constant. Charge can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transferred from one body to another.

Coulomb's law states that the electrostatic force between two stationary point charges is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Where $\epsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space ($\approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2\text{N}^{-1}\text{m}^{-2}$).
The electric field $\mathbf{E}$ at a point is defined as the electrostatic force $\mathbf{F}$ experienced by a small positive test charge $q_0$ placed at that point, divided by the charge itself.

blocks.caption: Figure 1.1: Electric field lines of an electric dipole (a pair of equal and opposite charges).

blocks.title: Electric Field Lines Rule
blocks.content: Electric field lines never cross each other. If they did, it would mean the electric field has two different directions at the point of intersection, which is physically impossible.
Electric flux $\Phi_E$ through a surface is the total number of electric field lines passing through that surface.

blocks.title: Gauss's Law
blocks.content:
The total electric flux through any closed surface is equal to $1/\epsilon_0$ times the net charge enclosed by the surface.

$$ \oint_S \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \frac{q_{\text{encl}}}{\epsilon_0} $$

items:
Infinitely Long Straight Charged Wire:
$$ E = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\epsilon_0 r} $$

Uniformly Charged Thin Spherical Shell (Radius R):
Outside ($r > R$): $$ E = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2} $$
Inside ($r < R$): $$ E = 0 $$
